was an [early modern](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") empire that ruled most of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). At its peak, the empire stretched from the outer fringes of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") Basin in the west, northern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in the northwest, and [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") in the north, to the [highlands](/wiki/Highland "Highland") of present-day [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") in the east, and the uplands of the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India").[[9]](#cite_note-Stein2010-12-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The **Mughal Empire** was an [early modern](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") empire that ruled most of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). At its peak, the empire stretched from the outer fringes of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") Basin in the west, northern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in the northwest, and [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") in the north, to the [highlands](/wiki/Highland "Highland") of present-day [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") in the east, and the uplands of the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India").[[9]](#cite_note-Stein2010-12-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The Mughal Empire is conventionally said to have been founded in 1526 by [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur"), a ruler from what is now [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), who with the help of the neighbouring [Safavid](/wiki/Safavid_Iran "Safavid Iran") and [Ottoman Empires](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"),[[11]](#cite_note-Gilbert2017-12) defeated the [sultan of Delhi](/wiki/Sultan_of_Delhi "Sultan of Delhi"), [Ibrahim Lodi](/wiki/Ibrahim_Lodi "Ibrahim Lodi"), in the [First Battle of Panipat](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Panipat "First Battle of Panipat") and swept down the plains of [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India"). The Mughal imperial structure, however, is sometimes dated to 1600, to the rule of Babur's grandson, [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar").[[12]](#cite_note-Stein2010-13) This imperial structure lasted until 1720, shortly after the death of the last major emperor, [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb"),[[13]](#cite_note-Stein2010-1-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) during whose reign the empire also achieved its maximum geographical extent. Reduced subsequently to the region in and around Old Delhi by 1760, the empire was formally dissolved by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") after the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857").

Although the Mughal Empire was created and sustained by military warfare,[[15]](#cite_note-Stein2010-2-16)[[16]](#cite_note-Robb2011-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Gilbert2017-2-18) it did not vigorously suppress the cultures and peoples it came to rule; rather, it equalised and placated them through new administrative practices,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006115-19)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb201199–100-20) and diverse ruling elites, leading to more efficient, centralised, and standardised rule.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006152–-21) The basis of the empire's collective wealth was agricultural taxes, implemented by the third Mughal emperor, Akbar.[[21]](#cite_note-Stein2010-3-22)[[22]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-1-23) These taxes, which amounted to well over half the output of a peasant cultivator,[[23]](#cite_note-Stein2010-4-24) were paid in the well-regulated silver currency,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006152–-21) and allowed peasants and artisans to enter larger markets.[[24]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-3-25)

The relative peace maintained by the empire during much of the 17th century was a factor in India's economic expansion.[[25]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-4-26) The burgeoning European presence in the Indian Ocean and an increasing demand for Indian raw and finished products generated much wealth for the Mughal court.[[26]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-5-27) There was [conspicuous consumption](/wiki/Conspicuous_consumption "Conspicuous consumption") among the Mughal elite,[[27]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-6-28) resulting in greater patronage of [painting](/wiki/Mughal_painting "Mughal painting"), literary forms, textiles, and [architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture"), especially during the reign of [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan").[[28]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-7-29) Among the Mughal [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Sites "UNESCO World Heritage Sites") in South Asia are [Agra Fort](/wiki/Agra_Fort "Agra Fort"), [Fatehpur Sikri](/wiki/Fatehpur_Sikri "Fatehpur Sikri"), [Red Fort](/wiki/Red_Fort "Red Fort"), [Humayun's Tomb](/wiki/Humayun%27s_Tomb "Humayun's Tomb"), [Lahore Fort](/wiki/Lahore_Fort "Lahore Fort"), [Shalamar Gardens](/wiki/Shalamar_Gardens,_Lahore "Shalamar Gardens, Lahore"), and the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal"), which has been described as "the jewel of Muslim art in India, and one of the universally admired masterpieces of the world's heritage".[[29]](#cite_note-Centre-30)

## Name

The word *Mughal* (also spelled *Mogul*[[30]](#cite_note-persianatemogul-31) or *Moghul* in English) is the Indo-Persian form of [*Mongol*](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols"). However, the Mughal dynasty's early followers were Chagatai Turks and not Mongols.[[31]](#cite_note-Hodgson-32)[[32]](#cite_note-Canfield-33) The term *Mughal* was applied to them in India by association with the Mongols and to distinguish them from the Afghan elite who ruled the Delhi Sultanate.[[31]](#cite_note-Hodgson-32) In the West, the term [*Mughal*](/wiki/Mughal_emperors "Mughal emperors") was used for the emperor and, by extension, the empire as a whole.[[33]](#cite_note-Fontana-34) The term remains disputed by [Indologists](/wiki/Indologists "Indologists").[[34]](#cite_note-RA-35) In [Marshall Hodgson's](/wiki/Marshall_Hodgson "Marshall Hodgson") view, the dynasty should be called *Timurid*/*Timuri* or *Indo-Timurid*.[[31]](#cite_note-Hodgson-32)

The closest to an official name for the empire was *[Hindustan](/wiki/Hindustan "Hindustan")*, which was documented in the [Ain-i-Akbari](/wiki/Ain-i-Akbari "Ain-i-Akbari").[[35]](#cite_note-Vanina-36) Mughal administrative records also refer to the empire as "dominion of Hindustan" (*Wilāyat-i-Hindustān*),[[36]](#cite_note-37) "country of Hind" (*Bilād-i-Hind*), "Sultanate of Al-Hind" (*Salṭanat(i) al-Hindīyyah*) as observed in the epitaph of Emperor [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb")[[37]](#cite_note-38) or endonymous identification from emperor [Bahadur Shah Zafar](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_Zafar "Bahadur Shah Zafar") as "Land of Hind" (*Hindostān*) in [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language").[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) Contemporary Chinese chronicles referred to the empire as *Hindustan* (*Héndūsītǎn*).[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The Mughal designation for their dynasty was *Gurkani* (*Gūrkāniyān*), a reference to their descent from the Turco-Mongol conqueror [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur"), who took the title *Gūrkān* 'son-in-law' after his marriage to a [Chinggisid](/wiki/Chinggisid "Chinggisid") princess.[[41]](#cite_note-Thackston2-42)

## History

See also: [Mughal dynasty](/wiki/Mughal_dynasty "Mughal dynasty")

### Babur and Humayun (1526–1556)

Main articles: [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") and [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Humayun_and_Babur_%28Late_Shah_Jahan_Album%29_Babur_detail.jpg/250px-Humayun_and_Babur_%28Late_Shah_Jahan_Album%29_Babur_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Humayun_and_Babur_(Late_Shah_Jahan_Album)_Babur_detail.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Joppen_map-India_in_1525_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg/250px-Joppen_map-India_in_1525_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joppen_map-India_in_1525_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)

Portrait of Babur in the *[Late Shah Jahan Album](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Late_Shah_Jahan_Album "commons:Category:Late Shah Jahan Album")*,[[42]](#cite_note-43) and map of India in 1525 just before the onset of Mughal rule

The Mughal Empire was founded by Babur (reigned 1526–1530), a Central Asian ruler who was descended from the [Turco-Mongol](/wiki/Turco-Mongol_tradition "Turco-Mongol tradition") conqueror [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur") (the founder of the [Timurid Empire](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire")) on his father's side, and from [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan") on his mother's side.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Paternally, Babur belonged to the [Turkicised](/wiki/Turkification "Turkification") [Barlas](/wiki/Barlas "Barlas") tribe of [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") origin.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Ousted from his ancestral domains in Central Asia, Babur turned to India to satisfy his ambitions.[[45]](#cite_note-46) He established himself in [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") and then pushed steadily southward into India from [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") through the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass").[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Babur's forces defeated [Ibrahim Lodi](/wiki/Ibrahim_Lodi "Ibrahim Lodi"), [Sultan of Delhi](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), in the [First Battle of Panipat](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Panipat "First Battle of Panipat") in 1526. Through his use of firearms and cannons, he was able to shatter Ibrahim's armies despite being at a numerical disadvantage,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards19958-47)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006116-48) expanding his dominion up to the mid [Indo-Gangetic Plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006117-49) After the battle, the centre of Mughal power shifted to [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards19958-47) In the decisive [Battle of Khanwa](/wiki/Battle_of_Khanwa "Battle of Khanwa"), fought near Agra a year later, the Timurid forces of Babur defeated the combined [Rajput](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") armies of [Rana Sanga](/wiki/Rana_Sanga "Rana Sanga") of [Mewar](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mewar "Kingdom of Mewar"), with his native cavalry employing traditional flanking tactics.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards19958-47)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006116-48)

The preoccupation with wars and military campaigns, however, did not allow the new emperor to consolidate the gains he had made in India.[[49]](#cite_note-50) The instability of the empire became evident under his son, [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun") (reigned 1530–1556), who was forced into exile in Persia by the rebellious [Sher Shah Suri](/wiki/Sher_Shah_Suri "Sher Shah Suri") (reigned 1540–1545).[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Humayun's exile in Persia established diplomatic ties between the [Safavid](/wiki/Safavid_dynasty "Safavid dynasty") and Mughal courts and led to increasing Persian cultural influence in the later restored Mughal Empire.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMajumdar197459,_65-51) Humayun's triumphant return from Persia in 1555 restored Mughal rule in some parts of India, but he died in an accident the next year.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards199512-52)

### Akbar to Aurangzeb (1556–1707)

Main articles: [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"), [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), and [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Jesuits_at_Akbar%27s_court.jpg/250px-Jesuits_at_Akbar%27s_court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jesuits_at_Akbar%27s_court.jpg)

[Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") holds a religious assembly of different faiths in the [Ibadat Khana](/wiki/Ibadat_Khana "Ibadat Khana") in Fatehpur Sikri.

[Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") (reigned 1556–1605) was born Jalal-ud-din Muhammad[[52]](#cite_note-53) in the [Umarkot Fort](/wiki/Umarkot_Fort "Umarkot Fort"),[[53]](#cite_note-54) to Humayun and his wife [Hamida Banu Begum](/wiki/Hamida_Banu_Begum "Hamida Banu Begum"), a [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") princess.[[54]](#cite_note-55) Akbar succeeded to the throne under a regent, [Bairam Khan](/wiki/Bairam_Khan "Bairam Khan"), who helped consolidate the Mughal Empire in India.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStein2010162-56) Through warfare, Akbar was able to extend the empire in all directions and controlled almost the entire Indian subcontinent north of the [Godavari River](/wiki/Godavari_River "Godavari River").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006128-57) He created a new ruling elite loyal to him, implemented a modern administration, and encouraged cultural developments. He increased trade with European trading companies.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) India developed a strong and stable economy, leading to commercial expansion and economic development.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Akbar allowed freedom of religion at his court and attempted to resolve socio-political and cultural differences in his empire by establishing a new religion, [Din-i-Ilahi](/wiki/Din-i-Ilahi "Din-i-Ilahi"), with strong characteristics of a ruler cult.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) He left his son an internally stable state, which was in the midst of its golden age, but before long signs of political weakness would emerge.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44)

[Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") (born Salim,[[57]](#cite_note-Mohammada-58) reigned 1605–1627) was born to Akbar and his wife [Mariam-uz-Zamani](/wiki/Mariam-uz-Zamani "Mariam-uz-Zamani"), an Indian princess.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Salim was named after the Indian Sufi saint, [Salim Chishti](/wiki/Salim_Chishti "Salim Chishti").[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) He "was addicted to opium, neglected the affairs of the state, and came under the influence of rival court cliques".[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Jahangir distinguished himself from Akbar by making substantial efforts to gain the support of the Islamic religious establishment. One way he did this was by bestowing many more *madad-i-ma'ash* (tax-free personal land revenue grants given to religiously learned or spiritually worthy individuals) than Akbar had.[[61]](#cite_note-62) In contrast to Akbar, Jahangir came into conflict with non-Muslim religious leaders, notably the [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") guru [Arjan](/wiki/Guru_Arjan "Guru Arjan"), whose execution was the first of many conflicts between the Mughal Empire and the Sikh community.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb201197–98-63)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006267-64)[[64]](#cite_note-BBC_Sikhs-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Khalili_Collection_Islamic_Art_mss_0874.3.jpg/250px-Khalili_Collection_Islamic_Art_mss_0874.3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khalili_Collection_Islamic_Art_mss_0874.3.jpg)

Group portrait of Mughal rulers, from [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") to [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb"), with the Mughal ancestor [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur") seated in the middle. On the left: [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") and Babur, with Abu Sa'id of Samarkand and Timur's son, [Miran Shah](/wiki/Miran_Shah "Miran Shah"). On the right: Aurangzeb, [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") and [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun"), and two of Timur's other offspring [Umar Shaykh](/wiki/Umar_Shaikh_Mirza_I "Umar Shaikh Mirza I") and [Muhammad Sultan](/wiki/Muhammad_Sultan_Mirza "Muhammad Sultan Mirza"). Created c. 1707–12.

[Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan") (reigned 1628–1658) was born to Jahangir and his wife [Jagat Gosain](/wiki/Jagat_Gosain "Jagat Gosain").[[57]](#cite_note-Mohammada-58) His reign ushered in the golden age of [Mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture").[[65]](#cite_note-66) During the reign of Shah Jahan, the splendour of the Mughal court reached its peak, as exemplified by the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal"). The cost of maintaining the court, however, began to exceed the revenue coming in.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Shah Jahan extended the Mughal Empire to the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") by ending the [Ahmadnagar Sultanate](/wiki/Ahmadnagar_Sultanate "Ahmadnagar Sultanate") and forcing the [Adil Shahis](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Bijapur "Sultanate of Bijapur") and [Qutb Shahis](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Golconda "Sultanate of Golconda") to pay tribute.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

Shah Jahan's eldest son, the liberal [Dara Shikoh](/wiki/Dara_Shikoh "Dara Shikoh"), became regent in 1658, as a result of his father's illness.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Dara championed a syncretistic Hindu-Muslim culture, emulating his great-grandfather Akbar.[[67]](#cite_note-68) With the support of the Islamic orthodoxy, however, a younger son of Shah Jahan, [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") (r. 1658–1707), seized the throne. Aurangzeb defeated Dara in 1659 and had him executed.[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) Although Shah Jahan fully recovered from his illness, Aurangzeb kept Shah Jahan imprisoned until he died in 1666.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruschke201768-69) Aurangzeb brought the empire to its greatest territorial extent,[[69]](#cite_note-70) and oversaw an increase in the Islamicisation of the Mughal state. He encouraged conversion to Islam, reinstated the *[jizya](/wiki/Jizya "Jizya")* on non-Muslims, and compiled the *[Fatawa 'Alamgiri](/wiki/Fatawa_%27Alamgiri "Fatawa 'Alamgiri")*, a collection of Islamic law. Aurangzeb also ordered the execution of the Sikh guru [Tegh Bahadur](/wiki/Guru_Tegh_Bahadur "Guru Tegh Bahadur"), leading to the militarisation of the Sikh community.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb201198-71)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006267-64)[[64]](#cite_note-BBC_Sikhs-65) From the imperial perspective, conversion to Islam integrated local elites into the king's vision of a network of shared identity that would join disparate groups throughout the empire in obedience to the Mughal emperor.[[71]](#cite_note-72) He led campaigns from 1682 in the Deccan,[[72]](#cite_note-EI2-73) annexing its remaining Muslim powers of Bijapur and Golconda,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995220–222-74)[[72]](#cite_note-EI2-73) though engaged in a [prolonged conflict](/wiki/Deccan_wars "Deccan wars") in the region which had a ruinous effect on the empire.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995252-75) The campaigns took a toll on the Mughal treasury, and Aurangzeb's absence led to a severe decline in governance, while stability and economic output in the Mughal Deccan plummeted.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995252-75)

Aurangzeb is considered the most controversial Mughal emperor,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006225-76) with some historians arguing his religious conservatism and intolerance undermined the stability of Mughal society,[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) while other historians question this, noting that he financed or patronised the building of non-Muslim institutions,[[76]](#cite_note-Copland2013-77) employed significantly more [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") in his imperial bureaucracy than his predecessors did, and opposed bigotry against Hindus and [Shia Muslims](/wiki/Shia_Muslims "Shia Muslims").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruschke201758-78)

### Decline (1707–1857)

Main article: [Decline of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Decline of the Mughal Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Farrukhsiyar_Procession_in_front_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Delhi.png/250px-Farrukhsiyar_Procession_in_front_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Delhi.png)](/wiki/File:Farrukhsiyar_Procession_in_front_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Delhi.png)

Delhi under the puppet-emperor [Farrukhsiyar](/wiki/Farrukhsiyar "Farrukhsiyar"). Effective power was held by the [Sayyid Brothers](/wiki/Sayyid_Brothers "Sayyid Brothers").

Aurangzeb's son, [Bahadur Shah I](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_I "Bahadur Shah I"), repealed the religious policies of his father and attempted to reform the administration. "However, after he died in 1712, the Mughal dynasty began to sink into chaos and violent feuds. In 1719 alone, four emperors successively ascended the throne",[[43]](#cite_note-Berndl-44) as figureheads under the rule of a brotherhood of nobles belonging to the [Indian Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India") caste known as the [Sadaat-e-Bara](/wiki/Barha_Dynasty "Barha Dynasty"), whose leaders, the [Sayyid Brothers](/wiki/Sayyid_Brothers "Sayyid Brothers"), became the de facto sovereigns of the empire.[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

During the reign of [Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah "Muhammad Shah") (reigned 1719–1748), the empire began to break up, and vast tracts of central India passed from Mughal to [Maratha](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy") hands. As the Mughals tried to suppress the independence of [Nizam-ul-Mulk, Asaf Jah I](/wiki/Nizam-ul-Mulk,_Asaf_Jah_I "Nizam-ul-Mulk, Asaf Jah I") in the Deccan, he encouraged the Marathas to invade central and northern India.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) The [Indian campaign](/wiki/Nader_Shah%27s_invasion_of_India "Nader Shah's invasion of India") of [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah"), who had previously reestablished [Iranian](/wiki/Afsharid_Iran "Afsharid Iran") [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") over most of West Asia, the Caucasus, and Central Asia, culminated with the [Sack of Delhi](/wiki/Sack_of_Delhi "Sack of Delhi") shattering the remnants of Mughal power and prestige, and taking off all the accumulated Mughal treasury. The Mughals could no longer finance the huge armies with which they had formerly enforced their rule. Many of the empire's elites now sought to control their affairs and broke away to form independent kingdoms.[[83]](#cite_note-84) But lip service continued to be paid to the Mughal Emperor as the highest manifestation of sovereignty. Not only the Muslim gentry, but the Maratha, Hindu, and Sikh leaders took part in ceremonial acknowledgements of the emperor as the sovereign of India.[[84]](#cite_note-MSA2-85)

Meanwhile, some regional polities within the increasingly fragmented Mughal Empire involved themselves and the state in global conflicts, leading only to defeat and loss of territory during conflicts such as the [Carnatic wars](/wiki/Carnatic_wars "Carnatic wars") and [Bengal War](/wiki/Bengal_War "Bengal War").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Joppen_map-India_in_1751_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg/250px-Joppen_map-India_in_1751_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joppen_map-India_in_1751_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)

The remnants of the empire in 1751

The Mughal Emperor [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II") (1759–1806) made futile attempts to reverse the Mughal decline. [Delhi was sacked](/wiki/Sack_of_Delhi_(1757) "Sack of Delhi (1757)") by the Afghans, and when the [Third Battle of Panipat](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_Panipat "Third Battle of Panipat") was fought between the Maratha Empire and the [Afghans](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire") (led by [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani")) in 1761, in which the Afghans were victorious, the emperor had ignominiously taken temporary refuge with the British to the east. In 1771, the Marathas [recaptured Delhi](/wiki/Capture_of_Delhi_(1771) "Capture of Delhi (1771)") from the [Rohillas](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Rohilkhand "Kingdom of Rohilkhand"), and in 1784 the Marathas officially became the protectors of the emperor in Delhi,[[85]](#cite_note-Rathod1994-86) a state of affairs that continued until the [Second Anglo-Maratha War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Maratha_War "Second Anglo-Maratha War"). Thereafter, the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") became the protectors of the Mughal dynasty in Delhi.[[84]](#cite_note-MSA2-85) The British East India Company took control of the former Mughal province of Bengal-Bihar in 1793 after it abolished local rule (Nizamat) that lasted until 1858, marking the beginning of the British colonial era over the Indian subcontinent. By 1857 a considerable part of former Mughal India was under the East India Company's control. After a crushing defeat in the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") which he nominally led, the last Mughal emperor, [Bahadur Shah Zafar](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_Zafar "Bahadur Shah Zafar"), was deposed by the British East India Company and exiled in 1858 to [Rangoon](/wiki/Rangoon "Rangoon"), Burma.[[86]](#cite_note-Conermann2015-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Portrait_of_Bahadur_Shah_II_as_calligrapher._Delhi%2C_ca._1850%2C_Royal_Ontario_Museum%2C_Toronto._-_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%8F.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Bahadur_Shah_II_as_calligrapher._Delhi%2C_ca._1850%2C_Royal_Ontario_Museum%2C_Toronto._-_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Bahadur_Shah_II_as_calligrapher._Delhi,_ca._1850,_Royal_Ontario_Museum,_Toronto._-_%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%B8%D1%8F.jpg)

Portrait of [Bahadur Shah Zafar](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_Zafar "Bahadur Shah Zafar")

### Causes of decline

Historians have offered numerous accounts of the several factors involved in the rapid collapse of the Mughal Empire between 1707 and 1720, after a century of growth and prosperity. A succession of short-lived incompetent and weak rulers, and civil wars over the succession, created political instability at the centre. The Mughals appeared virtually unassailable during the 17th century, but, once gone, their [imperial overstretch](/wiki/Imperial_overstretch "Imperial overstretch") became clear, and the situation could not be recovered. The seemingly innocuous European trading companies, such as the [British East Indies Company](/wiki/British_East_Indies_Company "British East Indies Company"), played no real part in the initial decline; they were still racing to get permission from the Mughal rulers to establish trades and factories in India.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

In fiscal terms, the throne lost the revenues needed to pay its chief officers, the emirs (nobles) and their entourages. The emperor lost authority as the widely scattered imperial officers lost confidence in the central authorities and made their deals with local men of influence. The imperial army bogged down in long, futile wars against the more aggressive [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), and lost its fighting spirit. Finally came a series of violent political feuds over control of the throne. After the execution of [Emperor Farrukhsiyar](/wiki/Farrukhsiyar "Farrukhsiyar") in 1719, local Mughal successor states took power in region after region.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

## Administration and state

Main article: [Government of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Government_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Government of the Mughal Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Joppen_map-India_in_1605_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg/250px-Joppen_map-India_in_1605_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joppen_map-India_in_1605_published_1907_by_Longmans.jpg)

India in 1605 and the end of emperor Akbar's reign; the map shows the different [subahs](/wiki/Subah "Subah"), or provinces, of his administration.

The Mughal Empire had a highly centralised, bureaucratic government, most of which was instituted during the rule of the third Mughal emperor, Akbar.[[89]](#cite_note-Robinson2009-90)[[72]](#cite_note-EI2-73) The central government was headed by the Mughal emperor; immediately beneath him were four ministries. The finance/revenue ministry, headed by an official called a *[diwan](/wiki/Divan "Divan")*, was responsible for controlling revenues from the empire's territories, calculating tax revenues, and using this information to distribute assignments. The ministry of the military (army/intelligence) was headed by an official titled *[mir bakhshi](/wiki/Mir_bakhshi "Mir bakhshi")*, who was in charge of military organisation, messenger service, and the *[mansabdari](/wiki/Mansabdari "Mansabdari")* system. The ministry in charge of law/religious patronage was the responsibility of the *sadr as-sudr,* who appointed judges and managed charities and stipends. Another ministry was dedicated to the imperial household and public works, headed by the *mir saman*. Of these ministers, the *diwan* held the most importance, and typically acted as the *[wazir](/wiki/Grand_Vizier_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Grand Vizier of the Mughal Empire")* (prime minister) of the empire.[[86]](#cite_note-Conermann2015-87)[[89]](#cite_note-Robinson2009-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)

### Administrative divisions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/A_moderate-sized_scan_of_the_map.jpg/250px-A_moderate-sized_scan_of_the_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_moderate-sized_scan_of_the_map.jpg)

[Ain-i-Akbari](/wiki/Ain-i-Akbari "Ain-i-Akbari") Subah, by Wilkinson, 1815

The empire was divided into *[Subah](/wiki/Subah "Subah")* (provinces), each of which was headed by a provincial governor called a *[subadar](/wiki/Subahdar "Subahdar").* The structure of the central government was mirrored at the provincial level; each *suba* had its own *[bakhshi](/wiki/Bakhshi_(Mughal_Empire) "Bakhshi (Mughal Empire)")*, *sadr as-sudr*, and finance minister that reported directly to the central government rather than the *subahdar*. *Subas* were subdivided into administrative units known as *[sarkars](/wiki/Sarkar_(administrative_division) "Sarkar (administrative division)"),* which were further divided into groups of villages known as *[parganas](/wiki/Pargana "Pargana")*. The Mughal government in the *pargana* consisted of a Muslim judge and local tax collector.[[86]](#cite_note-Conermann2015-87)[[89]](#cite_note-Robinson2009-90) *Parganas* were the basic administrative unit of the Mughal Empire.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

Mughal administrative divisions were not static. Territories were often rearranged and reconstituted for better administrative control, and to extend cultivation. For example, a *sarkar* could turn into a *subah*, and *Parganas* were often transferred between *sarkars*. The hierarchy of division was ambiguous sometimes, as a territory could fall under multiple overlapping jurisdictions. Administrative divisions were also vague in their geography—the Mughal state did not have enough resources or authority to undertake detailed land surveys, and hence the geographical limits of these divisions were not formalised and maps were not created. The Mughals instead recorded detailed statistics about each division, to assess the territory's capacity for revenue, based on simpler land surveys.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

### Capitals

The Mughals had multiple imperial capitals, established throughout their rule. These were the cities of [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), and [Fatehpur Sikri](/wiki/Fatehpur_Sikri "Fatehpur Sikri"). Power often shifted back and forth between these capitals.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinopoli1994294–295-94) Sometimes this was necessitated by political and military demands, but shifts also occurred for ideological reasons (for example, Akbar's establishment of Fatehpur Sikri), or even simply because the cost of establishing a new capital was marginal.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinopoli1994304–305-95) Situations where two simultaneous capitals existed happened multiple times in Mughal history. Certain cities also served as short-term, provincial capitals, as was the case with Aurangzeb's shift to [Aurangabad](/wiki/Aurangabad "Aurangabad") in the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan "Deccan").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinopoli1994294–295-94) [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") was the [summer capital](/wiki/Summer_capital "Summer capital") of Mughals from 1526 to 1681.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

The imperial camp, used for military expeditions and royal tours, also served as a kind of mobile, "de facto" administrative capital. From the time of Akbar, Mughal camps were huge in scale, accompanied by numerous personages associated with the royal court, as well as soldiers and labourers. All administration and governance were carried out within them. The Mughal Emperors spent a significant portion of their ruling period within these camps.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinopoli1994296_&_298-97)

After Aurangzeb, the Mughal capital definitively became the walled city of [Shahjahanabad](/wiki/Shahjahanabad "Shahjahanabad") (Old Delhi).[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Law

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Police_Chuprasi%2C_Delhi.png/250px-Police_Chuprasi%2C_Delhi.png)](/wiki/File:Police_Chuprasi,_Delhi.png)

Police in Delhi under Bahadur Shah II, 1842

The Mughal Empire's legal system was context-specific and evolved throughout the empire's rule. Being a Muslim state, the empire employed *[fiqh](/wiki/Fiqh "Fiqh")* (Islamic jurisprudence) and therefore the fundamental institutions of Islamic law such as those of the *[qadi](/wiki/Qadi "Qadi")* (judge), *[mufti](/wiki/Mufti "Mufti")* (jurisconsult), and *[muhtasib](/wiki/Muhtasib "Muhtasib")* (censor and market supervisor) were well-established in the Mughal Empire. However, the dispensation of justice also depended on other factors, such as administrative rules, local customs, and political convenience. This was due to Persianate influences on Mughal ideology and the fact that the Mughal Empire governed a non-Muslim majority.[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99) Scholar Mouez Khalfaoui notes that legal institutions in the Mughal Empire systemically suffered from the corruption of local judges.[[99]](#cite_note-Khalfaoui-100)

#### Legal ideology

The Mughal Empire followed the Sunni [Hanafi](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi") system of jurisprudence. In its early years, the empire relied on Hanafi legal references inherited from its predecessor, the Delhi Sultanate. These included the *[al-Hidayah](/wiki/Al-Hidayah "Al-Hidayah")* (the best guidance) and the *Fatawa al-Tatarkhaniyya* (religious decisions of the Emire Tatarkhan). During the Mughal Empire's peak, the *[Fatawa 'Alamgiri](/wiki/Fatawa_%27Alamgiri "Fatawa 'Alamgiri")* was commissioned by Emperor Aurangzeb. This compendium of Hanafi law sought to serve as a central reference for the Mughal state that dealt with the specifics of the South Asian context.[[99]](#cite_note-Khalfaoui-100)

The Mughal Empire also drew on Persian notions of kingship. Particularly, this meant that the Mughal emperor was considered the supreme authority on legal affairs.[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99)

#### Courts of law

Various kinds of courts existed in the Mughal Empire. One such court was that of the *qadi*. The Mughal *qadi* was responsible for dispensing justice; this included settling disputes, judging people for crimes, and dealing with inheritances and orphans. The *qadi* also had additional importance in documents, as the seal of the *qadi* was required to validate deeds and tax records. *Qadis* did not constitute a single position, but made up a hierarchy. For example, the most basic kind was the *[pargana](/wiki/Pargana "Pargana")* (district) *qadi*. More prestigious positions were those of the *qadi al-quddat* (judge of judges) who accompanied the mobile imperial camp, and the *qadi-yi lashkar* (judge of the army).[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99) *Qadis* were usually appointed by the emperor or the *sadr-us-sudr* (chief of charities).[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99)[[100]](#cite_note-101) The jurisdiction of the *qadi* was availed by Muslims and non-Muslims alike.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

The *[jagirdar](/wiki/Jagirdar "Jagirdar")* (local tax collector) was another kind of official approach, especially for high-stakes cases. Subjects of the Mughal Empire also took their grievances to the courts of superior officials, who held more authority and punitive power than the local *qadi*. Such officials included the *[kotwal](/wiki/Kotwal "Kotwal")* (local police), the *[faujdar](/wiki/Faujdar "Faujdar")* (an officer controlling multiple districts and troops of soldiers), and the most powerful, the *[subahdar](/wiki/Subahdar "Subahdar")* (provincial governor). In some cases, the emperor dispensed justice directly.[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99) Jahangir was known to have installed a "chain of justice" in the [Agra Fort](/wiki/Agra_Fort "Agra Fort") that any aggrieved subject could shake to get the attention of the emperor and bypass the inefficacy of officials.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Self-regulating tribunals operating at the community or village level were common, but sparse documentation of them exists. For example, it is unclear how *[panchayats](/wiki/Panchayats "Panchayats")* (village councils) operated in the Mughal era.[[98]](#cite_note-Chatterjee2019-99)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Economy of the Mughal Empire")

The Mughal economy was large and prosperous.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104)[[104]](#cite_note-Maddison2003-105) India was producing 24.5% of the world's manufacturing output up until 1750.[[105]](#cite_note-Williamson-106)[[104]](#cite_note-Maddison2003-105) Mughal India's economy has been described as a form of [proto-industrialisation](/wiki/Proto-industrialisation "Proto-industrialisation"), like that of 18th-century Western Europe before the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").[[106]](#cite_note-voss-107)

Modern historians and researchers generally agree that the character of the Mughal Empire's economic policy resembles the [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") system in dealing with trade and billions to achieve the economic ends.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJ.J.L._Gommans200275-108)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreusand2018"...Mughal_rulers_pursued_a_more_laissez-faire_economic_approach,_benefiting_from_the_prosperity_of..."-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAli2008-111)

The Mughals were responsible for building an extensive road system and creating a uniform currency.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995185–204-112) The empire had an extensive road network, which was vital to the economic infrastructure, built by a [public works](/wiki/Public_works "Public works") department set up by the Mughals which designed, constructed and maintained roads linking towns and cities across the empire, making trade easier to conduct.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104)

The main base of the empire's collective wealth was agricultural taxes, instituted by the third Mughal emperor, Akbar.[[21]](#cite_note-Stein2010-3-22)[[22]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-1-23) These taxes, which amounted to well over half the output of a peasant cultivator,[[23]](#cite_note-Stein2010-4-24) were paid in the well-regulated silver currency,[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot2006152–-21) and caused peasants and artisans to enter larger markets.[[24]](#cite_note-AsherTalbot2006-3-25) In circa 1595, Modern historians estimated the state's annual revenues of the Mughal Empire were around 99,000,000 rupees.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJorge_Flores201573-113)

### Coinage

Main article: [Mughal currency](/wiki/Mughal_currency "Mughal currency")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Coin_of_Aurangzeb%2C_minted_in_Kabul.jpg/250px-Coin_of_Aurangzeb%2C_minted_in_Kabul.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coin_of_Aurangzeb,_minted_in_Kabul.jpg)

Coin of Aurangzeb, minted in Kabul, dated 1691/2.

The Mughals adopted and standardised the [rupee](/wiki/Rupee "Rupee") (*rupiya*, or silver) and [dam](/wiki/Dam_(Indian_coin) "Dam (Indian coin)") (copper) currencies introduced by [Sur](/wiki/Sur_Empire "Sur Empire") Emperor [Sher Shah Suri](/wiki/Sher_Shah_Suri "Sher Shah Suri") during his brief rule.[[113]](#cite_note-114) The Mughals minted coins with high purity, never dropping below 96%, and without [debasement](/wiki/Debasement "Debasement") until the 1720s.[[114]](#cite_note-Richards2003-115)

Despite India having its stocks of gold and silver, the Mughals produced minimal gold of their own but mostly minted coins from imported [bullion](/wiki/Bullion "Bullion"), as a result of the empire's strong export-driven economy, with global demand for Indian agricultural and industrial products drawing a steady stream of [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal") into India.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995-116)

### Labour

The historian Shireen Moosvi estimates that in terms of contributions to the Mughal economy, in the late 16th century, the primary sector contributed 52%, the secondary sector 18% and the tertiary sector 29%; the secondary sector contributed a higher percentage than in early 20th-century [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"), where the secondary sector only contributed 11% to the economy.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoosvi2015433-117) In terms of the urban-rural divide, 18% of Mughal India's labour force were urban and 82% were rural, contributing 52% and 48% to the economy, respectively.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

According to Moosvi, Mughal India had a per-capita income, in terms of wheat, 1.24% higher in the late 16th century than British India did in the early 20th century.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoosvi2015432-119) This income, however, would have to be revised downwards if manufactured goods, like clothing, would be considered. Compared to food per capita, expenditure on clothing was much smaller though, so relative income between 1595 and 1596 should be comparable to 1901–1910.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoosvi2015450-120) However, in a system where wealth was hoarded by elites, wages were depressed for [manual labour](/wiki/Manual_labour "Manual labour").[[120]](#cite_note-moosvi-121) While [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") also existed, it was limited largely to household servants.[[120]](#cite_note-moosvi-121)

### Agriculture

Indian agricultural production increased under the Mughal Empire.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104) A variety of crops were grown, including food crops such as wheat, rice, and [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), and non-food [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop") such as cotton, [indigo](/wiki/Indigofera_tinctoria "Indigofera tinctoria") and [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"). By the mid-17th century, Indian cultivators began to extensively grow two new crops from the Americas, maize and tobacco.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104)

The Mughal administration emphasised the [agrarian reform](/wiki/Agrarian_reform "Agrarian reform") that began under the non-Mughal emperor Sher Shah Suri, which Akbar adopted and furthered with more reforms. The civil administration was organised hierarchically based on merit, with promotions based on performance.[[121]](#cite_note-122) The Mughal government funded the building of [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") systems across the empire, which produced much higher [crop yields](/wiki/Crop_yield "Crop yield") and increased the net revenue base, leading to increased agricultural production.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104)

A major Mughal reform introduced by Akbar was a new land revenue system called *zabt*. He replaced the [tribute](/wiki/Tribute "Tribute") system, previously common in India and used by [Tokugawa Japan](/wiki/Tokugawa_Japan "Tokugawa Japan") at the time, with a monetary tax system based on a uniform currency.[[114]](#cite_note-Richards2003-115) The revenue system was biased in favour of higher value cash crops such as cotton, indigo, [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane"), tree crops, and opium, providing state incentives to grow cash crops, in addition to rising market demand.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1995-116) Under the *zabt* system, the Mughals also conducted extensive [cadastral surveying](/wiki/Cadastral_surveying "Cadastral surveying") to assess the area of land under [plough](/wiki/Plough "Plough") cultivation, with the Mughal state encouraging greater land cultivation by offering tax-free periods to those who brought new land under cultivation.[[114]](#cite_note-Richards2003-115) The expansion of agriculture and cultivation continued under later Mughal emperors, including Aurangzeb.[[122]](#cite_note-Ludden96-123)

Mughal agriculture was in some ways advanced compared to European agriculture at the time, exemplified by the common use of the [seed drill](/wiki/Seed_drill "Seed drill") among Indian peasants before its adoption in Europe.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabibKumarRaychaudhuri1987214-124) Geared sugar [rolling mills](/wiki/Roller_mill "Roller mill") first appeared in Mughal India, using the principle of rollers as well as [worm gearing](/wiki/Worm_gear "Worm gear"), by the 17th century.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

### Industrial manufacturing

Further information: [Mughal Karkhanas](/wiki/Mughal_Karkhanas "Mughal Karkhanas")

South Asia during the Mughal's rule was a very fertile ground for manufacturing technologies coveted by the Europeans before the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrew_de_la_Garza2016114–115-126) Up until 1750, India produced about 25% of the world's industrial output.[[126]](#cite_note-williamson-127)

[Manufactured goods](/wiki/Manufactured_goods "Manufactured goods") and cash crops from the Mughal Empire were sold throughout the world.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104) The growth of manufacturing industries in the Indian subcontinent during the Mughal era in the 17th–18th centuries has been referred to as a form of [proto-industrialisation](/wiki/Proto-industrialisation "Proto-industrialisation"), similar to 18th-century Western Europe before the Industrial Revolution.[[106]](#cite_note-voss-107)

In [early modern Europe](/wiki/Early_modern_Europe "Early modern Europe"), there was significant demand for products from Mughal India, particularly cotton textiles, as well as goods such as spices, peppers, [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo"), silks, and [saltpetre](/wiki/Saltpetre "Saltpetre") (for use in [munitions](/wiki/Munitions "Munitions")).[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104) [European fashion](/wiki/1650%E2%80%931700_in_Western_European_fashion "1650–1700 in Western European fashion"), for example, became increasingly dependent on Mughal Indian textiles and silks.[[127]](#cite_note-Prakash-128)

#### Textile industry

See also: [Muslin trade in Bengal](/wiki/Muslin_trade_in_Bengal "Muslin trade in Bengal") and [Mughal clothing](/wiki/Mughal_clothing "Mughal clothing")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Renaldis_muslin_woman.jpg/250px-Renaldis_muslin_woman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Renaldis_muslin_woman.jpg)

Muslim Lady Reclining or An Indian Girl with a Hookah, painted in Dacca, 18th century.

The largest manufacturing industry in the Mughal Empire was [textile manufacturing](/wiki/Textile_manufacturing "Textile manufacturing"), particularly cotton textile manufacturing, which included the production of [piece goods](/wiki/Piece_goods "Piece goods"), [calicos](/wiki/Calico "Calico"), and [muslins](/wiki/Muslin "Muslin"). The cotton [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry") was responsible for a large part of the empire's international trade.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104) India had a 25% share of the global textile trade in the early 18th century,[[128]](#cite_note-129) and it represented the most important manufactured goods in world trade in the 18th century.[[129]](#cite_note-Parthasarathi-130) The most important centre of cotton production was the Bengal province, particularly around its capital city of [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka").[[130]](#cite_note-Eaton-131)

The production of cotton was advanced by the diffusion of the [spinning wheel](/wiki/Spinning_wheel "Spinning wheel") across India shortly before the Mughal era, lowering the costs of yarn and helping to increase demand for cotton. The diffusion of the [spinning wheel](/wiki/Spinning_wheel "Spinning wheel") and the incorporation of the [worm gear](/wiki/Worm_gear "Worm gear") and [crank](/wiki/Crank_(mechanism) "Crank (mechanism)") handle into the roller [cotton gin](/wiki/Cotton_gin "Cotton gin") led to greatly expanded Indian cotton textile production during the Mughal era.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

### Bengal Subah

Main article: [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah")

See also: [Muslin trade in Bengal](/wiki/Muslin_trade_in_Bengal "Muslin trade in Bengal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Boro_Katra_4_by_Ashif_Siddique.jpg/250px-Boro_Katra_4_by_Ashif_Siddique.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boro_Katra_4_by_Ashif_Siddique.jpg)

Ruins of the [Great Caravanserai](/wiki/Bara_Katra "Bara Katra") in [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka")

The Bengal Subah province was especially prosperous from the time of its takeover by the Mughals in 1590 until the British East India Company seized control in 1757.[[132]](#cite_note-133) Historian [C. A. Bayly](/wiki/C._A._Bayly "C. A. Bayly") wrote that it was probably the Mughal Empire's wealthiest province.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Domestically, much of India depended on Bengali products such as rice, silks and cotton textiles. Overseas, Europeans depended on Bengali products such as cotton textiles, silks, and opium.[[127]](#cite_note-Prakash-128) The province was a leading producer of grains, salt, fruits, liquors and wines, precious metals and ornaments.[[134]](#cite_note-Nanda-2005-135)

After 150 years of rule by Mughal [viceroys](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy"), Bengal gained de facto independence as a dominion under [Murshid Quli Khan](/wiki/Murshid_Quli_Khan "Murshid Quli Khan"), the first [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawab_of_Bengal "Nawab of Bengal") in 1717.[[135]](#cite_note-136) The Nawabs permitted European companies to set up trading posts across the region, which regarded Bengal as the richest place for trade.[[134]](#cite_note-Nanda-2005-135)

#### Shipbuilding industry

Mughal India had a large shipbuilding industry, which was also largely centred in the Bengal province. Economic historian Indrajit Ray estimates the shipbuilding output of Bengal during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries at 223,250 tons annually, compared with 23,061 tons produced in nineteen colonies in North America from 1769 to 1771.[[136]](#cite_note-ray174-137) He also assesses ship repairing as very advanced in Bengal.[[136]](#cite_note-ray174-137)

## Demographics

See also: [Demographics of India § History](/wiki/Demographics_of_India#History "Demographics of India")

### Population

India's population growth accelerated under the Mughal Empire, with an unprecedented economic and demographic upsurge which boosted the Indian population by 60%[[137]](#cite_note-mcevedy184-138) to 253% in 200 years during 1500–1700.[[138]](#cite_note-maddison236-139) The Indian population had a faster growth during the Mughal era than at any known point in [Indian history](/wiki/Indian_history "Indian history") before the Mughal era.[[104]](#cite_note-Maddison2003-105)[[137]](#cite_note-mcevedy184-138) By the time of Aurangzeb's reign, there were a total of 455,698 villages in the Mughal Empire.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabibKumarRaychaudhuri1987170-140)

The following table gives population estimates for the Mughal Empire, compared to the total population of South Asia including the regions of modern [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), and compared to the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population"):

| Year | Mughal Empire   population | Total Indian   population | % of South Asian   population | World   population | % of world   population |

The Jewish community in the empire engaged in trading jewelry and precious stones.[[141]](#cite_note-IshratAlam-142) [Sarmad Kashani](/wiki/Sarmad_Kashani "Sarmad Kashani") engaged in religious activities in the Mughal court.[[142]](#cite_note-Fischel-143)

### Urbanisation

According to [Irfan Habib](/wiki/Irfan_Habib "Irfan Habib"), cities and towns boomed under the Mughal Empire, which had a relatively high degree of urbanisation for its time, with 15% of its population living in urban centres.[[143]](#cite_note-Eraly2007-144) This was higher than the percentage of the urban population in contemporary Europe at the time and higher than that of [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in the 19th century;[[143]](#cite_note-Eraly2007-144) the level of urbanisation in Europe did not reach 15% until the 19th century.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

Under Akbar's reign in 1600, the Mughal Empire's urban population was up to 17 million people, 15% of the empire's total population. This was larger than the entire urban population in Europe at the time, and even a century later in 1700, the urban population of England, Scotland and Wales did not exceed 13% of its total population,[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabibKumarRaychaudhuri1987170-140) while British India had an urban population that was under 13% of its total population in 1800 and 9% in 1881, a decline from the earlier Mughal era.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabibKumarRaychaudhuri1987165-146) By 1700, Mughal India had an urban population of 23 million people, larger than British India's urban population of 22.3 million in 1871.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

Those estimates were criticised by [Tim Dyson](/wiki/Tim_Dyson "Tim Dyson"), who considers them exaggerations. According to Dyson, urbanisation of the Mughal Empire was less than 9%.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

The historian [Nizamuddin Ahmad](/wiki/Nizamuddin_Ahmad "Nizamuddin Ahmad") (1551–1621) reported that, under Akbar's reign, there were 120 large cities and 3200 townships.[[143]](#cite_note-Eraly2007-144) Several cities in India had a population between a quarter-million and half-million people,[[143]](#cite_note-Eraly2007-144) with larger cities including [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") (in [Agra Subah](/wiki/Agra_Subah "Agra Subah")) with up to 800,000 people, [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") (in [Lahore Subah](/wiki/Lahore_Subah "Lahore Subah")) with up to 700,000 people,[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHabibKumarRaychaudhuri1987171-149) [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") (in [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah")) with over 1 million people,[[149]](#cite_note-150) and Delhi (in [Delhi Subah](/wiki/Delhi_Subah "Delhi Subah")) with over 600,000 people.[[150]](#cite_note-Moosvi2008-151)

Cities acted as markets for the sale of goods, and provided homes for a variety of merchants, traders, shopkeepers, artisans, moneylenders, weavers, craftspeople, officials, and religious figures.[[103]](#cite_note-Schmidt2015-104) However, several cities were military and political centres, rather than manufacturing or commerce centres.[[151]](#cite_note-Chaudhuri2008-152)

## Culture

See also: [Indo-Persian culture](/wiki/Indo-Persian_culture "Indo-Persian culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/32/Mushafi-ghulam-hamdani.png)](/wiki/File:Mushafi-ghulam-hamdani.png)

[Ghulam Hamdani Mushafi](/wiki/Ghulam_Hamdani_Mushafi "Ghulam Hamdani Mushafi"), the poet first believed to have coined the name "*Urdu*" around 1780 AD for a language that went by a multiplicity of names before his time.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Generally, classical historiographies depicted the Mughal Empire's origin as a sedentarised agrarian society. However, modern historians such as [André Wink](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Wink "André Wink"), [Jos J. L. Gommans](/wiki/Jos_J._L._Gommans "Jos J. L. Gommans"), [Anatoly Khazanov](/wiki/Anatoly_Khazanov "Anatoly Khazanov"), Thomas J. Barfield, and others, argued the Mughals originated from nomadic culture.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaushik_RoyPeter_Lorge2014146-154) [Pius Malekandathil](/wiki/Pius_Malekandathil "Pius Malekandathil") argued instead that although it was true that the Mughal had their origin as nomadic civilisation, they became more sendentarised as time passed, as exemplified by their military tradition.[[154]](#cite_note-155) The Mughal Empire was definitive in the early-modern and modern periods of South Asian history, with its legacy in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh and Afghanistan seen in cultural contributions such as:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Mir_Taqi_Mir_1786.jpg/250px-Mir_Taqi_Mir_1786.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mir_Taqi_Mir_1786.jpg)

The Taj Mahal in the 1870s

* Centralised imperial rule that consolidated the smaller polities of South Asia.[[155]](#cite_note-156)
* The amalgamation of [Persian art](/wiki/Persian_art "Persian art") and literature with [Indian art](/wiki/Indian_art "Indian art").[[156]](#cite_note-157)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Badshahi_Mosque%2C_Lahore._King%27s_Mosque.jpg/250px-Badshahi_Mosque%2C_Lahore._King%27s_Mosque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Badshahi_Mosque,_Lahore._King%27s_Mosque.jpg)

Badshahi Mosque, [Lahore, Punjab](/wiki/Lahore,_Punjab "Lahore, Punjab"), Pakistan

* The development of [Mughlai cuisine](/wiki/Mughlai_cuisine "Mughlai cuisine"), an amalgamation of South Asian, Iranian and Central Asian culinary styles.
* The development of [Mughal clothing](/wiki/Mughal_clothing "Mughal clothing"), jewellery and fashion, utilising richly decorated fabrics such as muslin, silk, brocade and velvet.
* The influence of the Persian language over [Old Hindi](/wiki/Old_Hindi "Old Hindi") led to the development of the [Hindustani language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language").[[157]](#cite_note-158)
* The introduction of sophisticated Iranian-style waterworks and horticulture through [Mughal gardening](/wiki/Mughal_gardens "Mughal gardens").[[158]](#cite_note-159)
* The introduction of [Turkish baths](/wiki/Turkish_bath "Turkish bath") into the Indian subcontinent.
* The evolution and refinement of [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture") and [Indian architecture](/wiki/Indian_architecture "Indian architecture"), and, in turn, the development of later Rajput and Sikh palatial architecture. A famous Mughal landmark is the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal").
* The development of the [Pehlwani](/wiki/Pehlwani "Pehlwani") style of [Indian wrestling](/wiki/Indian_wrestling "Indian wrestling"), a combination of Indian [malla-yuddha](/wiki/Malla-yuddha "Malla-yuddha") and Persian [varzesh-e bastani](/wiki/Varzesh-e_bastani "Varzesh-e bastani").[[159]](#cite_note-Alter1992a-160)[[160]](#cite_note-Alter1992b-161)
* The construction of [Maktab](/wiki/Maktab_(education) "Maktab (education)") schools, where youth were taught the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") and [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") such as the *[Fatawa 'Alamgiri](/wiki/Fatawa_%27Alamgiri "Fatawa 'Alamgiri")* in their indigenous languages.
* The development of [Hindustani classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music "Hindustani classical music"),[[161]](#cite_note-162) and instruments such as the [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar").

### Customs

The procession of marriage among the royals of the Mughal Empire was recorded with many reports of extravagant gifts. One occasion was during the marriage of a son of emperor [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), [Salim](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"), with the daughter of a ruler of [Bijapur](/wiki/Bijapur "Bijapur"), [Raja Bhagwant Das](/wiki/Bhagwant_Das "Bhagwant Das"), where the gift presented by Bhagwant Das consisted of many horses, 100 elephants, many male and female slaves of [Abyssinian](/wiki/Habesha_peoples "Habesha peoples"), Caucasian, and native Indian origins, who brought with them various gold and silver utensils as [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry").[[162]](#cite_note-163)

### Architecture

Main articles: [Mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture") and [Yakhchāl](/wiki/Yakhch%C4%81l "Yakhchāl")

The Mughals made a major contribution to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") with the development of their distinctive architectural style. This style was derived from earlier [Indo-Islamic architecture](/wiki/Indo-Islamic_architecture "Indo-Islamic architecture") as well as from [Iranian](/wiki/Iranian_architecture "Iranian architecture") and [Central Asian](/wiki/Architecture_of_Central_Asia "Architecture of Central Asia") architecture (particularly [Timurid architecture](/wiki/Timurid_architecture "Timurid architecture")), while incorporating further influences from [Hindu architecture](/wiki/Hindu_architecture "Hindu architecture").[[163]](#cite_note-Petersen1996-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165) [Mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture") is distinguished, among other things, by [bulbous domes](/wiki/Onion_dome "Onion dome"), [ogive arches](/wiki/Pointed_arch "Pointed arch"), carefully-composed and polished façades, and the use of hard red sandstone and marble as construction materials.[[163]](#cite_note-Petersen1996-164)[[165]](#cite_note-166)

Furthermore, [William Dalrymple](/wiki/William_Dalrymple "William Dalrymple") mentioned that during the final days of the Mughal [fall of Delhi](/wiki/Siege_of_Delhi "Siege of Delhi") in 1857, an [ice house](/wiki/Ice_house_(building) "Ice house (building)") structure existed in Delhi.[[166]](#cite_note-167) Emperor Shah Jahan has recorded establishing an ice-house in [Sirmaur](/wiki/Sirmaur_district "Sirmaur district"), north of Delhi.[[167]](#cite_note-168)

Many monuments were built during the Mughal era by the Muslim emperors, especially [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), including the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal")—a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") considered "the jewel of Muslim art in India and one of the universally admired masterpieces of the world's heritage",[[29]](#cite_note-Centre-30) attracting 7–8 million unique visitors a year. The palaces, tombs, [gardens](/wiki/Mughal_garden "Mughal garden") and forts built by the dynasty stand today in [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra"), [Aurangabad](/wiki/Aurangabad,_Maharashtra "Aurangabad, Maharashtra"), [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka"), [Fatehpur Sikri](/wiki/Fatehpur_Sikri "Fatehpur Sikri"), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur "Jaipur"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul"), [Sheikhupura](/wiki/Sheikhupura "Sheikhupura"), and many other cities of India, Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh,[[168]](#cite_note-169) such as:

### Art and literature

Main articles: [Mughal painting](/wiki/Mughal_painting "Mughal painting") and [Mughal clothing](/wiki/Mughal_clothing "Mughal clothing")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Finial_in_the_Form_of_a_Parrot%2C_Mughal_empire.jpeg/250px-Finial_in_the_Form_of_a_Parrot%2C_Mughal_empire.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Finial_in_the_Form_of_a_Parrot,_Mughal_empire.jpeg)

Finial in the form of a parrot, Mughal Empire, 17th century

The [Mughal artistic tradition](/wiki/Mughal_painting "Mughal painting"), mainly expressed in painted miniatures, as well as small luxury objects, was eclectic, borrowing from Iranian, Indian, Chinese and Renaissance European stylistic and thematic elements.[[169]](#cite_note-170) Mughal emperors often took in Iranian bookbinders, illustrators, painters and calligraphers from the Safavid court due to the commonalities of their Timurid styles, and due to the Mughal affinity for Iranian art and calligraphy.[[170]](#cite_note-171) Miniatures commissioned by the Mughal emperors initially focused on large projects illustrating books with eventful historical scenes and court life, but later included more single images for albums, with portraits and animal paintings displaying a profound appreciation for the serenity and beauty of the natural world.[[171]](#cite_note-172) For example, Emperor Jahangir commissioned brilliant artists such as [Ustad Mansur](/wiki/Ustad_Mansur "Ustad Mansur") to realistically portray unusual flora and fauna throughout the empire.

The literary works Akbar and Jahangir ordered to be illustrated ranged from epics like the *[Razmnama](/wiki/Razmnama "Razmnama")* (a Persian translation of the Hindu epic, the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*) to historical memoirs or biographies of the dynasty such as the *[Baburnama](/wiki/Baburnama "Baburnama")* and *[Akbarnama](/wiki/Akbarnama "Akbarnama")*, and *[Tuzk-e-Jahangiri](/wiki/Tuzk-e-Jahangiri "Tuzk-e-Jahangiri")*. Richly finished albums (*[muraqqa](/wiki/Muraqqa "Muraqqa")*) decorated with calligraphy and artistic scenes were mounted onto pages with decorative borders and then bound with covers of stamped and gilded or painted and lacquered leather.[[172]](#cite_note-173) Aurangzeb (1658–1707) was never an enthusiastic patron of painting, largely for religious reasons, and took a turn away from the pomp and ceremonial of the court around 1668, after which he probably commissioned no more paintings.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/India%2C_Mughal%2C_early_17th_century_-_An_Illuminated_Folio_from_the_Royal_Manuscript_of_the_Farhang-i_Jahangiri_%28_-_2013.318.a_-_Cleveland_Museum_of_Art.tif/lossy-page1-250px-India%2C_Mughal%2C_early_17th_century_-_An_Illuminated_Folio_from_the_Royal_Manuscript_of_the_Farhang-i_Jahangiri_%28_-_2013.318.a_-_Cleveland_Museum_of_Art.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:India,_Mughal,_early_17th_century_-_An_Illuminated_Folio_from_the_Royal_Manuscript_of_the_Farhang-i_Jahangiri_(_-_2013.318.a_-_Cleveland_Museum_of_Art.tif)

### Language

Main articles: [Persian language in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Persian_language_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Persian language in the Indian subcontinent"), [Persian and Urdu](/wiki/Persian_and_Urdu "Persian and Urdu"), and [Hindustani language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language")

Though the Mughals were of [Turko-Mongol](/wiki/Turko-Mongol "Turko-Mongol") origin, their reign enacted the revival and height of the [Persian language](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") in the Indian subcontinent, and by the end of the 16th-century Turki (Chagatai) was understood by relatively few at court.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeyller2011329-175) Accompanied by literary patronage was the institutionalisation of Persian as an official and courtly language; this led to Persian reaching nearly the status of a first language for many inhabitants of Mughal India.[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177) Historian [Muzaffar Alam](/wiki/Muzaffar_Alam "Muzaffar Alam") argues that the Mughals used Persian purposefully as the vehicle of an overarching [Indo-Persian](/wiki/Indo-Persian "Indo-Persian") political culture, to unite their diverse empire.[[177]](#cite_note-178) Persian had a profound impact on the languages of South Asia; one such language, today known as [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), developed in the imperial capital of Delhi in the late Mughal era. It began to be used as a literary language in the Mughal court from the reign of [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), who described it as the language of his *[dastans](/wiki/Dastan "Dastan")* (prose romances) and replaced Persian as the informal language of the Muslim elite.[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180) According to contemporary poet [Mir Taqi Mir](/wiki/Mir_Taqi_Mir "Mir Taqi Mir"), "Urdu was the language of Hindustan by the authority of the King."[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

## Military

Further information: [Army of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Army_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Army of the Mughal Empire"), [Mughal weapons](/wiki/Mughal_weapons "Mughal weapons"), and [Mughal artillery](/wiki/Mughal_artillery "Mughal artillery")

### Gunpowder warfare

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Officer_of_the_Mughal_Army%2C_c.1585_%28colour_litho%29.jpg/250px-Officer_of_the_Mughal_Army%2C_c.1585_%28colour_litho%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Officer_of_the_Mughal_Army,_c.1585_(colour_litho).jpg)

Mughal [matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock") rifle, 16th century

See also: [Gunpowder empires](/wiki/Gunpowder_empires "Gunpowder empires") and [History of gunpowder: India and the Mughal Empire](/wiki/History_of_gunpowder#India_and_the_Mughal_Empire "History of gunpowder")

Mughal India was one of the three Islamic [gunpowder empires](/wiki/Gunpowder_empires "Gunpowder empires"), along with the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Safavid Persia](/wiki/Safavid_Persia "Safavid Persia").[[31]](#cite_note-Hodgson-32)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreusand2011-183)[[183]](#cite_note-184) By the time he was invited by [Lodi](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty") governor of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Daulat Khan](/wiki/Daulat_Khan_Lodi "Daulat Khan Lodi"), to support his rebellion against Lodi [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") [Ibrahim Khan](/wiki/Ibrahim_Lodi "Ibrahim Lodi"), [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") was familiar with [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") [firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") and [field artillery](/wiki/Field_artillery "Field artillery"), and a method for deploying them. Babur had employed Ottoman expert [Ustad Ali Quli](/wiki/Ustad_Ali_Quli "Ustad Ali Quli"), who showed Babur the standard Ottoman formation—artillery and firearm-equipped infantry protected by wagons in the centre and the [mounted archers](/wiki/Mounted_archer "Mounted archer") on both wings. Babur used this formation at the [First Battle of Panipat](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Panipat "First Battle of Panipat") in 1526, where the [Afghan](/wiki/Afghan_(ethnonym) "Afghan (ethnonym)") and [Rajput](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") forces loyal to the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), though superior in numbers but without the gunpowder weapons, were defeated. The decisive victory of the Timurid forces is one reason opponents rarely met Mughal princes in pitched battles throughout the empire's history.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreusand2011255-185) In India, guns made of [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") were recovered from [Calicut](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") (1504) and [Diu](/wiki/Diu,_India "Diu, India") (1533).[[185]](#cite_note-Partington226-186) [Fathullah Shirazi](/wiki/Fathullah_Shirazi "Fathullah Shirazi") (c. 1582), a Persian polymath and mechanical engineer who worked for Akbar, developed an early multi-gun shot. As opposed to the [polybolos](/wiki/Polybolos "Polybolos") and [repeating crossbows](/wiki/Repeating_crossbow "Repeating crossbow") used earlier in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and China, respectively, Shirazi's rapid-firing gun had multiple [gun barrels](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") that fired [hand cannons](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon") loaded with gunpowder. It may be considered a version of a [volley gun](/wiki/Volley_gun "Volley gun").[[186]](#cite_note-Fathullah_Shirazi-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/The_Padshahnama%2C_imperial_court_guards_and_nobles.jpg/250px-The_Padshahnama%2C_imperial_court_guards_and_nobles.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Padshahnama,_imperial_court_guards_and_nobles.jpg)

Mughal [musketeer](/wiki/Musketeer "Musketeer"), 17th century

By the 17th century, Indians were manufacturing a diverse variety of firearms; large guns, in particular, became visible in [Tanjore](/wiki/Tanjore "Tanjore"), [Dacca](/wiki/Dacca "Dacca"), [Bijapur](/wiki/Bijapur "Bijapur") and [Murshidabad](/wiki/Murshidabad "Murshidabad").[[187]](#cite_note-188)

### Rocketry and explosives

In the sixteenth century, [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") was the first to initiate and use metal cylinder [rockets](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") known as *bans*, particularly against [war elephants](/wiki/War_elephant "War elephant"), during the battle of Sanbal.[[188]](#cite_note-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190) In 1657, the [Mughal Army](/wiki/Army_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Army of the Mughal Empire") used rockets during the [siege of Bidar](/wiki/Siege_of_Bidar "Siege of Bidar").[[190]](#cite_note-Yazdani15-191) Prince Aurangzeb's forces discharged rockets and [grenades](/wiki/Grenade "Grenade") while scaling the walls. Sidi Marjan was mortally wounded when a rocket struck his large gunpowder depot, and after twenty-seven days of hard fighting, [Bidar](/wiki/Bidar "Bidar") was captured by the Mughals.[[190]](#cite_note-Yazdani15-191)

In *A History of Greek Fire and Gunpowder*, [James Riddick Partington](/wiki/James_Riddick_Partington "James Riddick Partington") described Indian rockets and [explosive](/wiki/Explosive_weapon "Explosive weapon") [mines](/wiki/Land_mine "Land mine"):[[185]](#cite_note-Partington226-186)

> The Indian war rockets ... were formidable weapons before such rockets were used in Europe. They had bam-boo rods, a rocket body lashed to the rod and iron points. They were directed at the target and fired by lighting the fuse, but the trajectory was rather erratic. The use of mines and counter-mines with explosive charges of gunpowder is mentioned for the times of Akbar and [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir").

## Science

A new curriculum for the [madrasas](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa") that stressed the importance of *uloom-i-muqalat* (Rational Sciences) and introduced new subjects such as [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), medicine, philosophy, and mathematics. The new curriculum produced a series of eminent scholars, engineers and architects.[[191]](#cite_note-192)[[192]](#cite_note-193)

### Astronomy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Jantar_Mantar._New_Delhi_%28Misra_Yantra%29._2010.jpg/250px-Jantar_Mantar._New_Delhi_%28Misra_Yantra%29._2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jantar_Mantar._New_Delhi_(Misra_Yantra)._2010.jpg)

[Jantar Mantar](/wiki/Jantar_Mantar "Jantar Mantar") in Delhi, built by [Jai Singh II](/wiki/Sawai_Jai_Singh "Sawai Jai Singh")

While there appears to have been little concern for [theoretical astronomy](/wiki/Theoretical_astronomy "Theoretical astronomy"), Mughal [astronomers](/wiki/Astronomers "Astronomers") made advances in [observational astronomy](/wiki/Observational_astronomy "Observational astronomy") and produced some *[Zij](/wiki/Zij "Zij")* treatises. Humayun built a personal [observatory](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") near Delhi. According to [Sulaiman Nadvi](/wiki/Sulaiman_Nadvi "Sulaiman Nadvi"), Jahangir and Shah Jahan intended to build observatories too, but were unable to do so. The [astronomical instruments](/wiki/Astronomical_instruments "Astronomical instruments") and observational techniques used at the Mughal observatories were mainly derived from [Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world").[[193]](#cite_note-Sharma-194)[[194]](#cite_note-Baber-195) In the 17th century, the Mughal Empire saw a synthesis between Islamic and [Hindu astronomy](/wiki/Hindu_astronomy "Hindu astronomy"), where Islamic observational instruments were combined with [Hindu computational](/wiki/Indian_mathematics "Indian mathematics") techniques.[[193]](#cite_note-Sharma-194)[[194]](#cite_note-Baber-195)

During the decline of the Mughal Empire, the Hindu king [Jai Singh II of Amber](/wiki/Jai_Singh_II_of_Amber "Jai Singh II of Amber") continued the work of Mughal [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"). In the early 18th century, he built several large observatories called [Yantra Mandirs](/wiki/Jantar_Mantar "Jantar Mantar"), to rival [Ulugh Beg](/wiki/Ulugh_Beg "Ulugh Beg")'s [Samarkand](/wiki/Samarkand "Samarkand") [observatory](/wiki/Ulugh_Beg_Observatory "Ulugh Beg Observatory"), and to improve on the earlier Hindu computations in the *[Siddhantas](/wiki/Surya_Siddhanta "Surya Siddhanta")* and Islamic observations in *[Zij-i-Sultani](/wiki/Zij-i-Sultani "Zij-i-Sultani")*. The instruments he used were influenced by Islamic astronomy, while the computational techniques were derived from Hindu astronomy.[[193]](#cite_note-Sharma-194)[[194]](#cite_note-Baber-195)

### Metallurgy

See also: [History of metallurgy in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/History_of_metallurgy_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "History of metallurgy in the Indian subcontinent") and [Mughal Karkhanas](/wiki/Mughal_Karkhanas "Mughal Karkhanas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Mughal_Celestial_Globe.jpg/250px-Mughal_Celestial_Globe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mughal_Celestial_Globe.jpg)

Celestial Globe by [Muhammad Saleh Thattvi](/wiki/Muhammad_Saleh_Thattvi "Muhammad Saleh Thattvi"), c.1663[[195]](#cite_note-196)

The society within the Mughal Empire operated the *Karkhanas*, which functioned as workshops for craftsmen. These Karkhanas were producing arms, ammunition, and also various items for the court and emperor's need such as clothes, shawls, turbans, jewelry, gold and silverware, perfumes, medicines, carpets, beddings, tents, and for the imperial stable-harnesses for the horses in irons, copper and other metals.[[196]](#cite_note-197)[[197]](#cite_note-198)[[198]](#cite_note-199)

Another aspect of the remarkable invention in Mughal India is the [lost-wax cast](/wiki/Lost-wax_casting "Lost-wax casting"), hollow, seamless, [celestial globe](/wiki/Celestial_globe "Celestial globe"). It was invented in [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") by Ali Kashmiri ibn Luqman in 998 [AH](/wiki/Anno_Hegirae "Anno Hegirae") (1589–90 CE). Twenty other such [globes](/wiki/Globe "Globe") were later produced in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") and Kashmir during the Mughal Empire. Before they were rediscovered in the 1980s, it was believed by modern [metallurgists](/wiki/Metallurgists "Metallurgists") to be technically impossible to produce hollow metal globes without any [seams](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/seam "wikt:seam").[[199]](#cite_note-200)
A 17th-century celestial globe was also made by Diya' ad-din Muhammad in Lahore, 1668 (now in Pakistan).[[200]](#cite_note-201)
